Q1: Honestly, my study routine was chaotic at best. I'm 27 and working to support myself while juggling 16 online courses, so I couldn't really establish a proper schedule. I'd squeeze in studying during lunch breaks, late at night after work, or whenever I had a spare moment. Most weeks I'd only manage about 7 hours total, which I know isn't enough, but that's all I could realistically do. I tried to listen to lectures while commuting since I learn better by hearing things anyway.
Q2: My pattern was completely dictated by deadlines and whatever crisis was happening that week. There was no regularity whatsoever—some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd be up until 3 AM trying to catch up. When assignments were due, I'd suddenly become very active, but otherwise I'd fall behind. It was exhausting living in this constant state of reaction rather than having any proactive plan.
Q3: Usually panic prompted me to log in—realizing a deadline was approaching or getting an email notification about something due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which honestly happened a lot given my 6% attendance rate. The gamified tasks they introduced actually surprised me and got me curious enough to check in a few extra times. But mostly it was fear of failing that drove me there.
Q4: I can't say it ever really became routine for me. There were brief periods where I'd try to log in every morning before work, but that never lasted more than a week. Life would always interrupt—extra shifts, family obligations, just being too exhausted to think. The closest I got to routine was checking discussion posts on Sunday nights, but even that was inconsistent.
Q5: The audio lectures and recorded sessions were absolutely essential for me because I'm an auditory learner. I could download them and listen while doing other things, which was the only way I could fit studying into my life. The discussion posts were also helpful because reading other students' perspectives sometimes clarified concepts faster than rereading materials myself. Those 111 minutes of video I watched were carefully selected based on what I thought would be on exams.
Q6: I ignored most of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs because I just didn't have time to sit and read. The interactive modules and extra practice exercises got skipped almost entirely. I knew they'd probably help, but when you're stretched this thin, you have to triage ruthlessly. If it wasn't directly connected to an assignment or exam, it got pushed aside indefinitely.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I'd be graded on or if I could complete it quickly. With limited hours, I needed immediate payoff. The gamified tasks actually surprised me because they made some content feel more accessible and less overwhelming. If I could see exactly how something would help my grade, I'd prioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones created anxiety because I knew I had to do them but often didn't have the time or energy. The optional stuff would sit there making me feel guilty, like a reminder of all the ways I wasn't being a proper student. Sometimes the gamified optional tasks were more appealing because they felt less intimidating than traditional assignments.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I downloaded audio files and listened to them constantly—on the bus, while cooking, while doing laundry. I'd take handwritten notes from those recordings and study from my notes without ever logging back into the LMS. I also used YouTube videos and free resources online when the course materials weren't clicking. A lot of my actual learning happened completely offline.
Q10: Most of my studying happened away from the LMS. I'd listen to a lecture once online, take notes, and then review those notes for hours over the following days. I'd discuss concepts with a friend who took similar courses, talking through problems verbally since that helps me understand better. None of that showed up anywhere. My exam score of 79.5 didn't come from thin air—it came from all that invisible work.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a lecture video once, download the audio if possible or take detailed notes, and then never return to that page. The LMS would show one view, but I might have engaged with that content ten more times through my notes or recordings. It was more efficient for me to work offline than to keep logging in repeatedly.
Q12: There were several points where I nearly gave up entirely. Mid-semester, my work increased my hours and I was running on four hours of sleep for weeks. My anxiety was through the roof—I'd feel physically sick thinking about how behind I was. The sheer number of courses I was taking became suffocating. I remember one week where I just couldn't bring myself to open the LMS at all because seeing all those incomplete tasks was too overwhelming.
Q13: Constantly. Fatigue was my biggest enemy—I'd log in intending to study but find myself unable to focus or retain anything. Work responsibilities meant I'd sometimes go days without touching coursework. When I was exhausted, I'd open the LMS, stare at the screen, and close it without accomplishing anything. The confusion from missing so many classes made it even harder because I'd feel lost and then give up faster.
Q14: Many times. I'd have the LMS open while doing other things, technically logged in but not actually absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd watch videos while half-asleep, which was basically useless. There were sessions where I'd click through materials just to feel like I was doing something, but my mind was completely elsewhere. Stress and anxiety made it hard to be present even when I was trying.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety would build up so much that starting felt impossible—like this wall I couldn't climb over. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was less tired, but tomorrow never came. Some tasks felt so overwhelming that avoiding them was easier than facing how far behind I was. It's a terrible cycle that I couldn't break.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that forced action. I'd ignore everything until a deadline was imminent, then scramble desperately. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates and flatlined otherwise. It wasn't healthy, but without that external pressure, I couldn't motivate myself given everything else I was dealing with. The panic of a deadline was sometimes the only thing stronger than my exhaustion.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. When I got good feedback, it surprised me and gave me a brief boost of motivation. But I often didn't check feedback promptly because I was afraid of what it might say. Negative feedback would spiral my anxiety worse, making me avoid the course more. I know that's counterproductive, but emotionally I couldn't handle criticism when I was already stretched so thin.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming with 16 courses running simultaneously. Having so many modules and deadlines across different courses meant I was always behind somewhere. The gamified elements they introduced were a pleasant surprise and made some tasks feel less daunting. But overall, the structure assumed students had more time and consistency than I could offer.
Q19: The flexibility of online courses was supposed to help but actually made it easier to fall behind since there was no fixed schedule holding me accountable. Courses with clearer, shorter modules were easier to engage with than ones with long, dense content. The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they broke things into smaller, more manageable pieces. But courses with lots of reading-heavy content were nearly impossible for me as an auditory learner.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying but struggling. They might be logging in, looking at materials, attempting to understand, but then hitting walls—confusion, anxiety, life circumstances—that prevent them from completing work. It could also be students who are exploring content out of genuine interest but can't translate that into finished assignments. High activity doesn't mean high capacity to produce.
Q21: That's essentially my story. My exam score was decent despite barely showing up in the LMS because I found alternative ways to learn that worked better for me. Auditory learning through downloaded materials, discussions with friends, handwritten notes—none of that registers online. Some students are efficient learners who don't need to spend hours in the system to understand the material.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when students are active and what they click on, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior or preferences for certain content types. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, emotional state, or life circumstances affecting participation. They can't distinguish between someone staring blankly at a screen and someone deeply focused. They measure presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting genuine mental effort into learning, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious, asking questions in my head, connecting ideas to things I already know. For me, engagement often happens when I'm listening to a lecture while walking, fully absorbed in the content. It's not about where you are or what you click—it's about whether your mind is actually working on the material.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was trying despite everything working against me. It can't capture the nights I fell asleep with my notes, the anxiety attacks before logging in, or how much I actually learned through methods that don't leave digital traces. It doesn't show that I'm a 27-year-old with limited resources working while studying, doing my absolute best with what I have. My low engagement numbers tell a story of absence, but the real story is one of struggle and persistence.
